_doc:
Maps high-miss-rate validator checks to the human-facing rule paragraph they enforce. The Rule Focus Summary uses this to surface which named rules a patch's diff is likely to invoke.

Selection rule: only checks with diff-only Gate-1 (no evidence_manifest dependency) AND a measured miss rate >= 25% on the historical corpus, OR a 100% miss rate at any volume, AND a Gate-1 predicate selective enough that surfacing it adds signal rather than noise.

Excluded despite high miss volume:
  - selector_cardinality_source_aware (101 misses / 36%): Gate-1 _ADD_SUPPORT_CONTEXT_RE matches nearly every Qcom patch (of_device_id / descriptor / `.data =` / `compatible =`). On Phase-1 corpus sweep it fired on 82% of recent patches; surfacing it on every brief is noise, not signal. Re-add only with a tighter Gate-1.

Historical miss rates (full corpus, 2026-04-27 to 2026-06-06): see focus_list_gain_analysis.md.

rules.headline: Touched-but-unsafe runtime-PM call
rules.why: Patch touches a function that calls pm_runtime_get_sync()/get without a balanced put_noidle on the failure path; the review must confirm bracket integrity or file the unbalanced reference.

rules.headline: Old DTB compatibility for new compatible/property
rules.why: New compatible/property added to a binding without explaining old-DTB / new-kernel behaviour; the review must show the legacy DTB still parses or document the break.

rules.headline: Binding companion-property dependency
rules.why: New binding property requires (or invalidates) a sibling property; the review must surface the companion dependency or add a `dependencies:` / `if-then` block.

rules.headline: Aggregate-vs-per-element scale audit
rules.why: Per-element bandwidth/rate is divided by a container/fabric width while the element struct exposes its own same-dimension field; the review must compare per-element width or prove homogeneity.

rules.headline: Level-IRQ re-enable without clearing source
rules.why: Threaded IRQ exits with `enable_irq()` while the hardware source remains asserted; level-triggered IRQs will retrigger continuously.

rules.headline: Provider `#*-cells` `const:` requirement
rules.why: New provider binding declares `#*-cells` without `const:` matching the driver xlate; permissive parent schemas allow consumers that the driver will reject at runtime.

rules.headline: Cross-instance raw pointer across independent unbind
rules.why: Driver stores a raw pointer into a sibling instance that is independently sysfs-unbindable; needs a quoted lifetime guarantee (suppress_bind_attrs, refcount, device_link, or coordinated teardown).

rules.headline: Managed device-link manual remove
rules.why: device_link_remove() called on a DL_FLAG_AUTOREMOVE_CONSUMER/SUPPLIER link; managed links are released by the driver core, not the driver.

rules.headline: Retained dynamic-object cleanup must clear pointer
rules.why: Static descriptor stores a dynamically-allocated framework object; every cleanup path that frees the object must also clear the retained pointer or probe-retry/rebind reuses freed state.

rules.headline: Alternate-path state reset for mode-dependent field
rules.why: Patch assigns a mode/type/sel/config field on one operational path while an alternate path (TPG, loopback, internal source) can reach the consumer without resetting it; the review must prove the alternate path resets the field or the consumer is guarded.

rules.headline: Macro arithmetic on unvalidated zero-capable input
rules.why: GENMASK(count-1, 0) or division/shift by a DT-parsed/variant-provided value that can be zero; the review must prove bounds validation or file the degenerate-input finding.

rules.headline: Branch-precedence / condition-widening diversion
rules.why: Patch widens an if/else-if guard with a new `||` disjunct and reorders the chain so an input that used to fall to a later side-effectful arm is now captured by an earlier arm; the review must trace which input changes arms and prove the bypassed arm's side effect (PHY/power init, lock, state flag, setup) is still performed.

rules.headline: Read-path widening of a writer-locked pointer
rules.why: Patch widens an `if (ctx->ptr)` block to add new dereferences of the same field, while a sibling teardown function (HPD/work/IRQ/timer/disconnect) frees the field under a lock the read-path function does not hold; the review must name the writer's free site and the lock asymmetry, or quote a lifetime guarantee on the field — pattern-alignment / API-correctness arguments do not establish lifetime.

trigger_obligations.obligation_text: For each new compatible string, verify it is a concrete hardware identifier, not a wildcard/family placeholder (`x`, `xx`, `*`) or a bus/transport suffix masquerading as hardware identity. If it is generic, require a specific compatible plus proven fallback or file a finding.
For each new driver `.compatible`, verify a matching binding accepts the same concrete string and that any missing match data is intentional for this hardware variant.
Treat `wsa885x`-style family/wildcard naming as suspect: prove it is an official exact part identifier or ask for specific part-number compatibles.

If this binding also uses `allOf:`/`$ref:`, verify closure uses `unevaluatedProperties: false`; otherwise file a DT schema closure finding. Do not mark `$ref` plus `additionalProperties: false` SAFE unless you prove the referenced schema contributes no properties.
For every referenced common schema in `allOf:`, name the referenced schema and verify the local property-closure mode does not reject properties evaluated by that schema.
For every `$ref:`, state whether it contributes properties outside the local `properties:` block and whether `unevaluatedProperties: false` is required.

For each newly required `#*-cells` property, audit every compatible in the binding's enum/oneOf and prove each compatible's driver/header actually provides that resource. If only some do, require an if/then carve-out or file [CONCERN].

When a binding with multiple compatibles references a provider common schema such as qcom,gcc.yaml, audit inherited required/provider cells (for example `#reset-cells`) per compatible, not only cells added in the local `required:` list.

When adding a `$ref` branch under `anyOf`, prove the new branch is compatible-gated or otherwise cannot validate unrelated vendor nodes; if loose closure can mask a stricter sibling schema, file a finding.
When adding a `$ref` branch under `oneOf`, verify the branch selection is mutually exclusive for compatible strings and cannot create ambiguous or fallback validation.
If a newly referenced vendor branch has `additionalProperties: true`, check whether it annotates properties for unrelated compatibles and bypasses parent `unevaluatedProperties: false`.

For an IRQ-like `*-gpios` property, verify whether the line is a hardware interrupt. If yes, require standard `interrupts`/`interrupts-extended` and IRQ consumer APIs; only mark SAFE if the line is genuinely used as GPIO state/control and that hardware reason is documented.
A `gpiod_to_irq()` consumer is evidence that the binding may be modeling an interrupt as GPIO. Cross-check the binding property and file a finding unless a documented GPIO-control reason exists.

For any DT `init-table`/register table property, verify whether cells are raw register addresses/values. Raw register scripts in DT are a finding unless the binding exposes semantic board parameters with units/ranges or proves the table is not hardware programming data.
Text describing register/value pairs in DT is evidence of an opaque hardware script. Require semantic properties or driver-side tables keyed by compatible.

For every added DTS node with a unit-address (@hex), compare its unit address numerically against existing same-parent siblings before and after the insertion anchor. Unit-addresses must be strictly ascending within each parent node.

For every new or changed .is_enabled callback, prove the framework cannot call it before/without .enable, or prove all resources needed for its register/MMIO reads are already active. Include bootloader-already-on count synchronization.
Expand regulator_is_enabled_regmap() to its regmap_read(); verify the register is accessible when the callback can run and that required clocks/domains are active.
If status can report hardware already enabled, prove Linux-side clk prepare/enable counts were acquired before any disable_unprepare path can run.
For each GENMASK(n-1, 0) or BIT(n) where n comes from DT/firmware/match-data/variant, prove n is validated > 0 and within bounds before the macro call. Unguarded zero → undefined shift → file [BUG].
For each shift where the shift count comes from an external input (DT property, firmware, register read), prove it is range-checked. Unchecked → potential undefined behavior → file [CONCERN].
For each division/modulo/array-index using a parsed count/width/rate, prove the value is validated non-zero before use. Zero divisor or OOB index → file [BUG].
For child/dummy platform devices using `PLATFORM_DEVID_NONE`, prove the name is globally unique or require `PLATFORM_DEVID_AUTO`/driver-specific naming.

If a child framework object callback uses memory allocated with parent devres, prove child teardown/drain happens before parent devres frees that memory; otherwise file a circular lifetime/UAF finding.

For each `devm_regmap_init*()` regmap, search the same driver/component for `regmap_exit()` or `snd_soc_component_exit_regmap()`. If present, verify devres cleanup is cancelled; otherwise file a double-cleanup/UAF finding.

Read `snd_soc_component_exit_regmap()` semantics: it calls `regmap_exit()`. If the regmap came from `devm_regmap_init*()`, this is not symmetric cleanup; it is manual free plus later devres free. Also check IRQ/work/control callbacks that can reach the regmap between component remove and devres release.

Record devres registration order for IRQ, component registration, regmap, GPIO, and regulator actions. If a component remove callback frees a resource while an earlier devm IRQ remains registered, file a teardown-window UAF finding.
For devm ASoC component registration, verify component `.remove` does not manually release resources still owned by longer-lived devres actions such as IRQ or regmap cleanup.
For every copy from firmware/framework RX storage, prove the actual RX length is bounded by the caller destination and matches the typed expected size before fields are consumed.
If protocol commands allow zero-length payloads, ensure `memcpy`/`memmove` is skipped when source or destination can be NULL even with length zero.
State TX size, requested RX size, actual RX length source, and whether same-buffer TX/RX reuse can leave stale TX bytes on short replies.
For each `SOC_*_EXT` control, inspect its `.put` callback: it must return 1 when the value changes and 0 only when unchanged. It must compare old vs new value before assignment.
For each custom `struct snd_kcontrol` `.put`, verify return value semantics, range validation, and old-value comparison. A callback that always returns 0 after assignment is a finding.

For cached volume/gain state, list all writers and all hardware consumers. If active playback should update immediately, require `.put` to program hardware; if intentionally latched, require a documented latch point. Snapshot before multi-register writes.
For cached mode/slot state consumed by DAI callbacks, prove ASoC/core or driver locking serializes controls against stream callbacks, or file an unlocked cached-state concern.
For cached slot-mask state consumed by DAI callbacks, prove serialization/snapshotting across control writes and stream reads; otherwise file a race or inconsistent-programming concern.
For remote-tracking governors, prove observed frequencies are not wrongly PM-QoS clamped, are rounded/validated against OPPs before stats/trace updates, and do not require `profile->target`.
Map every new governor pointer read against governor_store/remove/module-unload writers and prove the reader holds the required lock/ref or file a race/UAF finding.
Check whether governor removal/module unload can bypass immutable governor protection and allow rebinding to an incompatible governor.
When polling depends on `get_cur_freq`, verify governor START rejects devices missing the callback instead of allowing repeated monitor failures.
For every RCG with `.hw_clk_ctrl = true` in this hunk, name the RCG, classify as hardware-triggered or software-controlled, and cite sibling evidence. AHB/XO/software-controlled RCG → file [BUG].

For every parent_data[] entry in this hunk, verify each P_*_OUT_EVEN/OUT_ODD/OUT_AUX maps to a dedicated clk_alpha_pll_postdiv struct, NOT the main PLL .clkr.hw directly. Shared .hw = wrong rate → file [BUG].

For each `qcom_cc_desc` in this hunk, compare .use_rpm against the nearest sibling desc (e.g. camcc-milos.c). If absent while sibling has it and this controller has GDSCs/PLL writes, file [BUG]: register access to unpowered block.
This hunk references .use_rpm. Verify it is present in the qcom_cc_desc. If the desc omits it, check sibling and file [BUG] if they all have it.
